```{r}
smry_txt <- capture.output(tweedie_model3)

# Extract the line containing "Tweedie power estimate"
power_line <- grep("Tweedie power estimate", smry_txt, value = TRUE)

# Extract the numeric value using regex
power3 <- as.numeric(sub(".*Tweedie power estimate:\\s*", "", power_line))

dispersion3 <- exp(tweedie_model3$fit$parfull["betadisp"])
aic_val3 <- AIC(tweedie_model3)
bic_val3 <- BIC(tweedie_model3)

model_tweedie3 <- tbl_regression(
  tweedie_model3,
  exponentiate = TRUE,
  intercept = TRUE
) 


tweedie_model_sq3 <- glmmTMB(RPQ13~ Mechanism_of_TBI + Extracranial_trauma + Ethicity + z_Age +z_Age2,
                     data = dat,
                     family = tweedie(link = "log"))

tweedie_model_sq3_glance<-glance(tweedie_model_sq3)
Tweedie_model_sq3 <- tbl_regression(tweedie_model_sq3, exponentiate = TRUE, intercept = TRUE )
model_tweedie_sq3<-glance(tweedie_model_sq3) 

# Capture summary output
smry_txt <- capture.output(tweedie_model_sq3)

# Extract the line containing "Tweedie power estimate"
power_line <- grep("Tweedie power estimate", smry_txt, value = TRUE)

# Extract the numeric value using regex
power_sq3 <- as.numeric(sub(".*Tweedie power estimate:\\s*", "", power_line))

dispersion_sq3  <- exp(tweedie_model_sq3$fit$parfull["betadisp"])
aic_val_sq3  <- AIC(tweedie_model_sq3)
bic_val_sq3  <- BIC(tweedie_model_sq3)

model_tweedie_sq3 <- tbl_regression(
  tweedie_model_sq3 ,
  exponentiate = TRUE,
  intercept = TRUE
) 


tweedie3 <- tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(model_tweedie3, model_tweedie_sq3),
  tab_spanner = c("**Tweedie with linear age**", "**Tweedie with quadratic age**")
) %>% as_flex_table() %>% autofit %>% 
  add_footer_lines(values = paste0(
    "Tweedie with linear age — Dispersion (φ): ", Sround(dispersion3, 2),
    "; Power (p): ", Sround(power3, 3),
    "; AIC: ", Sround(aic_val3, 2),
    "; BIC: ", Sround(bic_val3, 2))
  ) %>%
  add_footer_lines(values = paste0(
    "Tweedie with quadratic age — Dispersion (φ): ", Sround(dispersion_sq3, 2),
    "; Power (p): ", Sround(power_sq3, 3),
    "; AIC: ", Sround(aic_val_sq3, 2),
    "; BIC: ", Sround(bic_val_sq3, 2))
  )


modfit3 <- bind_rows(model_ols_glance3, model_cat_glance3, tweedie_model3_glance, tweedie_model_sq3_glance) %>% 
mutate(Model_type = c("Linear", "Linear categorical ethnicity",  "Tweedie", "Tweedie square age")) %>%
      relocate(Model_type, .before = 1) %>% mutate(model=3, paper_ID=paper_ID)

 
```

Treating ethnicity as a continuous variable was not appropriate, as the differences between groups were not equally spaced and the relationship with the outcome was not linear. As categorical variable there was a small decrease in AIC of `r Sround(model_ols_glance3$AIC - model_cat_glance3$AIC,2)`.

```{r}

linearmodel3
```

Fitting a Tweedie model to account for zero's, the estimated dispersion parameter (φ) was `r  Sround(dispersion3, 2)`, indicating greater variability than would be expected under a Gaussian model, and the Tweedie power parameter (p) was `r  Sround(power3, 2)`, consistent with a compound Poisson–Gamma distribution. Compared to the linear model, the Tweedie model yielded a substantial AIC reduction of `r Sround(model_ols_glance3$AIC-aic_val3,2)`. Inclusion of a squared age term marginally improved model fit (AIC and BIC) and residual diagnostics, these improvements were small, and the simpler model was retained.

In this model, the intercept represents the geometric mean of the RPQ13 score for patients who were white, had not been assaulted, had not experienced extracranial trauma, and were at the average age (since age was standardized). This corresponds to the expected RPQ13 score when all predictors are at their reference level or zero. The exponentiated coefficient for Mechanism_of_TBI indicates that patients who had been assaulted had an estimated 17% higher RPQ13 score compared to those who had not (p = 0.321). The coefficient for standardized age was 0.88, suggesting that the expected RPQ13 score decreases by 12% as age increases by one standard deviation, although this was not statistically significant.

```{r}
tweedie3

```

The residual diagnostics for the final Tweedie model appeared satisfactory, with the DHARMa package producing appropriate residual plots to assess overdispersion, zero inflation, and uniformity. DHARMa residuals are based on the rank of each observed value among many simulated values from the fitted model. This approach produces discrete residual values (e.g., 0.25, 0.5, 0.75) that reflect the percentile position of the observed value within the simulated distribution.

```{r}
# Simulate residuals using DHARMa
sim_res <- simulateResiduals(tweedie_model3, plot = TRUE)
```

#### Inferential Reproducibility conclusion

This model was not found to be inferentially reproducible, due to differences in model distribution and specification. A Tweedie distribution was identified as more appropriate for the data, given the presence of zero inflation and non-normality. In addition, model parameterisation differed, including treating categorical variables as continuous.

```{r}

Assumptions_data<- bind_rows(M1Rassump, M2Rassump,M3Rassump) 
write.csv(Assumptions_data,file="data/Assumptions_data22.csv")
write.csv(fdat,file="data/model_data22.csv")
modfit<- bind_rows(modfit1, modfit2,modfit3) 
write.csv(modfit, file="data/modfit_22.csv")


```
:::
